- Observed result after fix: the temporary hook captured exactly one real `agent:turn:end` event from a live ACPX/Codex turn with `success: true`; the pre-runtime max-concurrency failure returned `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` and captured zero `agent:turn:end` events for that blocked session.
- What was not tested: production gateway deployment. This was intentionally run against a temporary local Gateway/config/state using source code from this branch.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the proof uses local Codex auth copied into a temporary Codex home and a one-off local proof harness that is not committed; hook handlers are internal and only observable when an operator installs/enables a hook.

## Testing
- Real source-built ACPX/Codex turn proof described above
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts`

target.title: fix(plugins): guard plugin tool descriptor accessors
target.body:
## Summary

- Treat unreadable plugin tool required fields as malformed before wrapping, caching, or exposing the tool.
- Keep valid sibling plugin tools available when one tool has hostile `name`, `execute`, `description`, or descriptor-cache accessors.
- Make effective tool inventory tolerate unreadable optional display metadata and fall back to raw descriptions instead of crashing doctor/inventory paths.

Related hardening context:

- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/88994
- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/89013
- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/89016

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: A plugin tool with hostile descriptor accessors could crash plugin tool resolution, descriptor caching, or effective tool inventory after earlier shape checks, causing healthy sibling tools or doctor/inventory output to disappear.

Real environment tested: Local focused regression tests plus AWS Crabbox changed-gate proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/agents/tools-effective-inventory.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SKIP_LOCK=1 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.json src/plugins/tools.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/agents/tools-effective-inventory-build.ts src/agents/tools-effective-inventory.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/plugins/tools.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/agents/tools-effective-inventory-build.ts src/agents/tools-effective-inventory.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed"`

Evidence after fix:

- Focused Vitest passed: 21 agent inventory tests and 79 plugin tests.
- Static checks passed: oxlint, oxfmt, and `git diff --check`.
- Direct repros changed from throwing hostile descriptor/display metadata getters to returning safe descriptors or inventory fallbacks.
- Autoreview: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.
- Crabbox: provider `aws`, run `run_2f40fa77f3e2`, lease `cbx_cc78e0a83171`, lanes `core, coreTests`, exit `0`.

Observed result after fix: Malformed plugin tools with unreadable required fields are isolated with diagnostics, valid siblings continue loading, optional descriptor/display metadata no longer crashes descriptor caching or inventory, and required descriptor-cache fields cannot be cached as empty fallbacks.

What was not tested: Blacksmith Testbox could not be used because the local Blacksmith CLI was not authenticated, so the broad changed gate ran through AWS Crabbox instead.

target.title: fix #82685: [Bug]: web_fetch fails to extract full HTML body content
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #82685 by treating title-only Readability output as failed body extraction instead of returning only the page title.
- Preserves the configured-provider-before-raw-HTML order for title-only Readability pages, so existing provider routing remains the first recovery path when configured.
- Hardens the runtime body scanner so fake `<body>` tags inside comments, raw-text containers, and inert/nested `<template>` regions do not replace the real visible document body.
- Keeps the change scoped to the `web_fetch` runtime extraction path; shared HTML-to-markdown helpers, provider configuration, network policy, and public API shape are unchanged.

What problem does this PR solve?

`web_fetch` could accept Readability output that only contained the page title as successful body extraction. In that state, users and agents could receive only the title instead of visible page content. The runtime body scanner also needed to preserve the document-body boundary while skipping HTML regions that can legally contain literal fake body markup but are not the visible body.

Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is user-visible: downstream tool output is misleading when `web_fetch` returns title-only text or hidden/inert fake body content instead of the actual page body.

What is the intended outcome?

This is a root-cause extraction-state fix: title-only Readability output no longer completes extraction, and body-boundary scanning now enforces the browser-visible body invariant before raw HTML text is produced. Successful behavior is provider output when a configured provider handles the title-only page, or visible body text from local raw HTML cleanup when no provider output is available.

What is intentionally out of scope?

This PR does not change shared `htmlToMarkdown`, provider configuration, network/auth/security policy, migrations, schema, public API shape, or the provider catalog. It only changes the `web_fetch` runtime extraction decision and focused regression coverage for that path.

What should reviewers focus on?

Please review the source-of-truth boundary between local Readability extraction, configured provider routing, and runtime-local raw HTML body cleanup. The provider compatibility boundary is preserved: title-only pages still try configured provider routing before local raw HTML cleanup, while scanner hardening only affects the local cleanup path when it is reached.

## Linked context

- Closes #82685.
- Updates PR #89028 after review feedback called out missing real-behavior proof, raw-text/template scanner gaps, and provider compatibility risk.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `web_fetch` no longer returns only the page title when Readability emits title-only text; it continues extraction and returns visible body content when no configured provider output is available.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw `web_fetch` tool execution with the real Readability extractor and a deterministic HTML fetch fixture. External network access was not required for this proof.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a Node proof script that calls the real Readability extractor, then calls `createWebFetchTool().execute` with `{ extractMode: 'markdown' }` against the same title-only fixture with provider fetching disabled.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

  ```text
  web_fetch title-only fallback proof
  fixture url: https://fixture.openclaw.test/title-only-template-raw-text
  readability.title: "Sample Page"
  readability.text: "Sample Page"
  readability.titleOnly: true
  web_fetch.status: 200
  web_fetch.contentType: text/html
  web_fetch.extractor: raw-html
  web_fetch.title.hasSamplePage: true
  web_fetch.text.hasBodyMarker: true
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the fixture forced title-only Readability output, and with no configured provider output available, the `web_fetch` result used `raw-html` with the expected body marker present.
- What was not tested: the exact original reporter page, because the issue did not include a concrete URL or full HTML fixture.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the live proof uses a deterministic fixture so the title-only condition and expected body marker are reproducible. The configured-provider order is covered by a focused regression test that asserts provider output wins before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the new focused regressions failed before this fix: the nested-template raw-text case surfaced fake script body text, and the provider-order guard caught the intermediate regression where local raw HTML cleanup ran before the configured provider.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "ignores fake body tags in raw-text content inside inert templates"` ŌĆö passed after the nested-template/raw-text scanner fix.
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "tries provider fallback before body HTML when readability returns only the page title"` ŌĆö passed after restoring provider-first order; configured provider output wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts` ŌĆö passed, 62 tests.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added coverage for fake body tags inside raw-text content nested within inert templates, proving the scanner returns the real body marker and excludes the fake body text.
- Restored title-only/provider coverage so configured provider output is used before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The nested-template raw-text regression initially returned fake script body text instead of the real body marker.
- The provider-order guard caught an intermediate regression where recoverable local body HTML bypassed the configured provider.

If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; focused regression coverage was added/updated for the scanner gap and provider compatibility boundary.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. For title-only Readability pages, `web_fetch` now continues extraction instead of stopping at the title. Configured provider order is preserved, and local raw HTML body cleanup is used only when provider output is unavailable.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No expansion of capability. The change stays inside `web_fetch` extraction and does not broaden network access, auth, secrets, or tool execution.

What is the highest-risk area?

Compatibility for title-only pages: `web_fetch` now treats title-only Readability as incomplete and may proceed to configured provider routing or local raw HTML cleanup instead of returning only the title.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The canonical source of truth remains `web_fetch` runtime extraction: Readability first, configured provider routing second, local raw HTML cleanup last for HTML extraction failures.
- The change is limited to title-only Readability output; normal successful Readability output is unchanged.
- The focused provider regression asserts that configured provider output still wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- Raw HTML cleanup stays runtime-local to `web_fetch`; shared conversion helpers and public API shape are unchanged.
- Related open PR scan result: PR #89028 is the active repair path for #82685, and current main did not already contain this title-only/body-boundary fix when this update started.
- Patch-quality note: the added `null`/`undefined` parser results are explicit scanner miss signals for malformed/non-tag input, not symptom masking; they allow scanning to continue until the real body boundary is found or no body is present.
- Size note: the diff is confined to one runtime file and its focused tests; the line count is mostly regression coverage for existing review findings in this PR.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after the updated branch is submitted and CI settles.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Maintainer: review the title-only extraction behavior with provider-first compatibility preserved.
- CI: rerun on the updated PR branch after submission.
- External proof: no exact reporter URL/HTML was available, so proof uses representative deterministic HTML that reproduces the title-only/body-cleanup invariant.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001/RF-008 fixed: raw-text content inside inert/nested templates is skipped while scanning body boundaries.
- RF-002/RF-004/RF-006 fixed: PR body now includes copied real `web_fetch` tool output for the title-only/body-cleanup behavior and describes the linked issue limitation.
- RF-003/RF-005 fixed: provider-first order is preserved for configured providers, addressing ClawSweeper's regression finding about local raw HTML cleanup bypassing configured provider routing.
- RF-007 fixed: author-side proof and regression-test gaps are addressed in this update.

target.title: ci: run QA Lab smoke profile on pull requests
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `QA Lab Smoke Profile`, a GitHub workflow that can run QA Lab evidence on GitHub runners and upload `.artifacts/qa-e2e/` for review.
- Keeps `workflow_dispatch` as the operator-facing path for the existing taxonomy-backed `smoke-ci` profile and optional UX Matrix `scenario_id` override.
- Adds a path-scoped `pull_request` self-proof trigger for this workflow file; PR runs execute one deterministic native QA scenario (`gateway-smoke`) so checkout, QA runtime build, QA command execution, summary generation, and artifact upload are proven before merge.
- Preserves blank manual `scenario_id` so manual dispatch with default inputs runs `qa run --qa-profile smoke-ci`; only the `pull_request` proof path defaults to `gateway-smoke`.
- Intentionally avoids editing existing release/smoke workflows, adding mobile simulator/emulator lanes, using live provider secrets, or changing QA Lab runtime behavior.
- This is intentionally **draft workflow wiring** because maintainers may still choose to fold this command shape into the planned smoke-ci workflow instead of landing a standalone workflow.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #94276
Related #94283
Related #94306

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Requested as a draft follow-up slice for GitHub Action / smoke-profile wiring after the QA Lab UX Matrix scenario work, with the caveat that the final smoke-ci integration may be renamed or folded into maintainer-owned smoke-ci work.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: QA Lab evidence can run on GitHub-hosted runners through this workflow, with artifacts uploaded from `.artifacts/qa-e2e/`. Manual dispatch keeps the `smoke-ci` profile path and `scenario_id` override for the UX Matrix scenario from PR 3.
- Real environment tested: GitHub Actions `pull_request` run on `ubuntu-24.04`, PR head `37e02e32ca751b9ae8ce4241b3786f7f8adf4351`, workflow run `27768692670`, job `82162374496`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
  - `PATH="/Users/solvely/.local/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH" node scripts/check-workflows.mjs`
  - `go run github.com/rhysd/actionlint/cmd/actionlint@v1.7.11 .github/workflows/qa-lab-smoke-profile.yml`
  - GitHub Actions PR run: `QA Lab Smoke Profile / Run QA Lab smoke profile`, https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27768692670/job/82162374496
- Evidence after fix: GitHub runner proof and linked artifact:

```text
Run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27768692670
Job: Run QA Lab smoke profile (82162374496)
Artifact: qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1
Artifact link: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27768692670/artifacts/7727264532

Runner steps:
- Checkout selected ref: success
- Setup Node environment: success
- Build private QA runtime: success
- Run QA evidence lane: success
- Summarize QA artifacts: success
- Upload QA evidence artifacts: success

QA command from the runner log:
pnpm openclaw qa suite --provider-mode mock-openai --scenario gateway-smoke --concurrency 1 --output-dir .artifacts/qa-e2e/gateway-smoke --fast

Runner output:
QA suite report: /home/runner/work/openclaw/openclaw/.artifacts/qa-e2e/gateway-smoke/qa-suite-report.md
QA suite evidence: /home/runner/work/openclaw/openclaw/.artifacts/qa-e2e/gateway-smoke/qa-evidence.json
QA suite summary: /home/runner/work/openclaw/openclaw/.artifacts/qa-e2e/gateway-smoke/qa-suite-summary.json

Downloaded artifact contents:
qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1/gateway-smoke/qa-evidence.json
qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1/gateway-smoke/qa-suite-report.md
qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1/gateway-smoke/qa-suite-summary.json
qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1/gateway-smoke/vitest/gateway-smoke.log
qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1/gateway-smoke/vitest/qa-evidence.json

qa-suite-summary.json:
- total scenarios: 1
- passed: 1
- failed: 0
- scenario: Gateway smoke evidence
- runner: vitest
- provider mode: mock-openai
- fast mode: true
- node: v24.16.0
```

- Observed result after fix: The PR-triggered workflow path exercised checkout, private QA runtime build, `qa suite --scenario gateway-smoke`, summary generation, and artifact upload on GitHub Actions. The uploaded artifact is the visible proof surface for the changed behavior.
- Manual dispatch routing fix: blank `workflow_dispatch` `scenario_id` now remains empty, so the shell falls through to `qa run --qa-profile <qa_profile>`; a non-empty manual `scenario_id` still runs `qa suite --scenario <scenario_id>`; `pull_request` alone supplies `gateway-smoke` for lightweight self-proof.
- What was not tested: The manual `workflow_dispatch` full `smoke-ci` profile path still cannot be directly dispatched until this workflow exists on the repository default branch. The UX Matrix scenario was not run because PR 3 has not provided its final scenario ID in this PR.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The PR proof lane intentionally runs one deterministic mock-provider scenario for speed and reliability. It does not use live provider secrets, Android emulators, iOS simulators, or generated screenshot/video media. The linked Actions artifact is the shiny proof artifact for this workflow change.
- Before evidence: A direct `gh workflow run qa-lab-smoke-profile.yml ...` attempt failed with GitHub's default-branch workflow lookup limitation while the workflow file existed only on the PR branch.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `PATH="/Users/solvely/.local/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH" node scripts/check-workflows.mjs`
- `go run github.com/rhysd/actionlint/cmd/actionlint@v1.7.11 .github/workflows/qa-lab-smoke-profile.yml`
- GitHub Actions `QA Lab Smoke Profile` PR run `27768692670`: success, artifact `qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1` uploaded.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

No runtime tests were added. This PR adds workflow/config wiring and proves it by running an existing QA Lab native scenario on a GitHub runner.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The first manual dispatch proof attempt failed because GitHub only exposes `workflow_dispatch` workflows that already exist on the repository default branch. The path-scoped PR trigger gives pre-merge runner proof for this workflow file. A later review found that blank manual `scenario_id` would incorrectly fall through to `gateway-smoke`; this revision preserves blank manual `scenario_id` so default manual dispatch reaches `qa run --qa-profile smoke-ci`.

If no test was added, why not?

The changed surface is a GitHub workflow. The meaningful proof is a successful GitHub Actions run and uploaded `.artifacts/qa-e2e/` evidence artifact, which is now present on the current head.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No runtime config or migration behavior changed. This adds a GitHub workflow with manual dispatch plus a path-scoped PR self-proof trigger.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, narrowly: a new workflow can run QA Lab commands on GitHub-hosted runners with read-only contents permissions, persisted checkout credentials disabled, and live provider keys scrubbed.

What is the highest-risk area?

Workflow ownership/shape may overlap with maintainer-owned smoke-ci work.

How is that risk mitigated?

The workflow is draft, does not rename or replace existing workflows, uses `smoke-ci` for manual dispatch by default, exposes `scenario_id` as the minimal UX Matrix integration target, and its automatic PR proof trigger is limited to changes to `.github/workflows/qa-lab-smoke-profile.yml`.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

ClawSweeper re-review requested after preserving manual dispatch routing and refreshing the successful GitHub runner proof on head `37e02e32ca751b9ae8ce4241b3786f7f8adf4351`.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Waiting on maintainer direction for dedicated workflow vs smoke-ci fold-in, and on PR 3 to provide the final UX Matrix scenario ID.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Fixed the manual dispatch routing bug: default blank `scenario_id` no longer turns manual dispatch into `gateway-smoke`.
- Preserved the PR self-proof path and re-proved it with Actions run `27768692670` plus uploaded artifact `qa-lab-smoke-profile-27768692670-1`.
- Kept the PR workflow-only on current `origin/main` and did not reintroduce gallery UI or UX Matrix scenario code.

target.title: feat(qa): crabline channel driver
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes QA profile channel drivers executable and separates channel identity from transport mode.

- Adds the merged `crabline` package as a QA Lab dependency pinned to `openclaw/crabline@93b4de9b933b2c32f16f265725a0af3c429997e2`.
- Runs the `smoke-ci` QA profile through the Crabline runtime via `import("crabline")`, with `channel: telegram` and other requested channel IDs resolved through Crabline instead of an inlined local fallback.
- Adds a separate `QA Smoke CI` PR workflow for the canonical mock-channel smoke profile.
- Keeps `.github/workflows/qa-live-transports-convex.yml` focused on scheduled/manual live transport coverage.
- Makes the `release` live profile fan out to the native Matrix, Telegram, Discord, Slack, and WhatsApp live lane catalogs, then merges their `qa-evidence.json` outputs into one profile evidence artifact.

## Why

Tests should be able to ask for a channel shape such as `telegram` while the selected profile decides whether that exchange is handled by Crabline or by a real live transport. `smoke-ci` is the PR CI mock-channel profile. Live service coverage stays in the live transport lanes and release profile evidence.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check .github/workflows/qa-live-transports-convex.yml .github/workflows/qa-smoke-ci.yml docs/concepts/qa-e2e-automation.md extensions/qa-lab/package.json pnpm-workspace.yaml extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsgo --noEmit -p tsconfig.extensions.json`
- `pnpm vitest run extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`

## Summary
- allow embedded preflight compaction to re-enter the same-process transcript write lock
- add a regression guard so compaction's lock acquisition keeps `allowReentrant: true`

## Why
When a visible chat turn times out near the context ceiling, a same-gateway transcript lock can still be settling. Auto/preflight compaction then triggers but fails with `session file locked`, causing channels like Telegram to surface a generic error instead of compacting and continuing.

This keeps the default non-reentrant lock behavior everywhere else; only compaction opts into the same logical-writer path already used by other transcript mutation helpers.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Preflight compaction should recover a full Telegram-topic transcript even when the previous same-gateway turn timed out and left a same-process transcript write lock settling. The user-visible behavior should be a completed OpenClaw response, not repeated generic Telegram errors.

- **Real environment tested:** Live OpenClaw gateway on Linux, Telegram topic session key `agent:main:telegram:group:-1003907348684:topic:8`, active install `/home/plexusadmin/.openclaw/openclaw-green`, model override `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7`. Secrets/tokens omitted.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Observed the affected Telegram topic repeatedly return generic errors.
  2. Checked session metadata and confirmed the topic was at/over its context window.
  3. Checked gateway logs and found preflight compaction triggering but failing on `session file locked` for the live gateway PID.
  4. Applied the same source change from this PR to the running package: compaction lock acquisition includes `allowReentrant: true`.
  5. Restarted/verified the gateway and ran an exact-session health check against the affected Telegram topic.
  6. Swept the sessions directory for leftover `*.jsonl.lock` files.

- **Evidence after fix:**

  Before fix, live gateway log:

  ```text
  Preflight compaction required but failed: session file locked (timeout 60000ms): pid=587038 /home/plexusadmin/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/2026-06-01T04-03-06-636Z_4092c331-5473-48c3-b973-df8b70600414.jsonl.lock
  ```

  Session context state at failure:

  ```json
  {
    "modelProvider": "github-copilot",
    "model": "claude-opus-4.7",
    "totalTokens": 144084,
    "contextTokens": 144000,
    "totalTokensFresh": true
  }
  ```

  After applying this fix to the running package, exact-topic smoke result:

  ```json
  {
    "status": "ok",
    "summary": "completed",
    "text": "TOPIC8_HOTFIX_OK",
    "provider": "github-copilot",
    "model": "claude-opus-4.7",
    "total": 39386
  }
  ```

  Lock sweep after verification:

  ```bash
  find /home/plexusadmin/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions -maxdepth 1 -name '*.jsonl.lock'
  # no output
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The affected Telegram topic responded successfully after the compaction lock change. The gateway remained running and no stuck transcript lock files remained after verification.

- **What was not tested:** I did not merge/deploy this PR as an upstream OpenClaw release. The live package has a temporary equivalent hotfix until this PR or an equivalent release lands.

## Test plan
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact-lock.test.ts src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts`
  - 4 files passed, 108 tests passed
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact-lock.test.ts`
  - 0 warnings, 0 errors

target.title: fix(gateway): force exit on zombie shutdown + 503 healthz during shutdown
target.body:
## Summary

On rh-bot.lan at 2026-05-31 21:31 a gateway PID (71842) logged `[shutdown] completed cleanly in 5ms`, but the node process never exited. It held port 18789 for 2h13m. Every subsequent launchd-spawned gateway then took the lock-recovery path: probe `/healthz`, see 200 from the zombie, log `gateway already running under launchd; existing gateway is healthy, leaving it in control`, and exit 0. launchd's `KeepAlive.SuccessfulExit=false` kept new gateways down. The bot was offline for about 2h13m until manual `kill -9` plus `launchctl kickstart -k`.

The original SIGKILL of the parent process is the separate root cause documented in memory `project_rh_bot_isolated_session_sigkill.md` (`openclaw isolated-session` spawn pattern). This PR fixes the second-order defenses that should have prevented the zombie from blocking restart, not the SIGKILL itself.

## Layer 1: force exit after every clean shutdown

`src/gateway/server-close.ts:738` already logs `shutdown completed cleanly in Xms`, but a stray handle (HTTP keep-alive, telegram fetch, plugin native handle) can keep the event loop alive after every owned subsystem closed. `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts:382` has an existing `armForceExitTimer(SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT_MS)` safety net, but it only arms for `runAcceptedRequest` (signal-driven), not for the `closeOnStartupFailure` path in `src/gateway/server.impl.ts:1059`. That gap is exactly the rh-bot incident shape.

This PR adds `armGatewayPostShutdownExitWatchdog` in `server-close.ts`. After "completed cleanly" is logged, an unref'd `setTimeout` is armed (default 5_000ms, configurable via `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_POST_SHUTDOWN_EXIT_TIMEOUT_MS`). When it fires:
- emit a structured `shutdownLog.warn` with the active-handle constructor summary (via `process._getActiveHandles` or `getActiveResourcesInfo`)
- record the `gateway.shutdown.zombie_detected` restart trace event for OTel/Loki
- call `process.exit(0)`

Because the timer is unref'd, a healthy natural exit always wins. The watchdog only fires when something else holds the loop open. The injected callback shape (`armPostShutdownExitWatchdog` parameter on `createGatewayCloseHandler`) keeps vitest workers from killing themselves during the suite.

## Layer 2: 503 on /healthz during shutdown, plus tighter preflight

The deployed `handleGatewayProbeRequest` in `src/gateway/server-http.ts` returns 200 unconditionally for `/healthz`. A zombie still holding its HTTP listener therefore answers 200, and `probeGatewayHealthz` in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts:369` accepts it as healthy (success criterion was `statusCode < 500`). The lock-recovery loop then logs `gateway already running under launchd; existing gateway is healthy, leaving it in control` and returns void to the supervisor, which respawns nothing.

This PR adds a module-level shutting-down flag in `server-close.ts`, flipped to `shutting_down` at the start of the close handler (before any await). `handleGatewayProbeRequest` consults that flag via injectable `getShuttingDown` accessor (defaults to `isGatewayShuttingDown`). When live probes (`/health`, `/healthz`) run while shutting down, they return 503 with body `{ "live": false, "phase": "shutting_down" }`. A `gateway.healthz.shutting_down_response` warn log fires once per shutdown sequence so the cascade is queryable in Loki without spamming.

`probeGatewayHealthz` is tightened to accept only `statusCode === 200`. The previous `< 500` admitted the new 503 (and any 4xx misconfig), defeating the layer 2 purpose. The lock-recovery loop now also emits `gateway.preflight.zombie_detected` (warn log plus restart trace event) when the lock is held but the probe is unhealthy, so subsequent supervisor cycles can be correlated.

The flag resets at the start of `startGatewayServer` so the in-process restart path returns to a `running` state before `/healthz` starts answering 200 again.

## OTel / Loki instrumentation

All four events emit via the existing `gatewayLog`/`shutdownLog`/`gatewayProbeLog` subsystems plus `recordGatewayRestartTrace`:

- `gateway.shutdown.zombie_detected` (Layer 1 trace) when the post-shutdown watchdog fires
- `gateway.healthz.shutting_down_response` (Layer 2 warn log) when /healthz returns 503 during shutdown
- `gateway.preflight.zombie_detected` (Layer 2 warn log + trace) when supervised lock recovery sees a held lock but an unhealthy probe
- the existing `restart.close.total` trace already covers clean-path shutdown completion

No new export plumbing is needed; the existing diagnostics-otel / Loki shipper consumes these events.

## Verification

Local Vitest with the patch applied:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-http.probe.test.ts src/gateway/probe.close-drain.test.ts src/gateway/server.preauth-hardening.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.supervised-lock.test.ts
Test Files  4 passed (4)
     Tests  59 passed (59)
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  10 passed (10)
```

`pnpm build` clean. `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --only=core --threads=4` clean. `node scripts/check-dynamic-import-warts.mjs` shows only the pre-existing `mcp-cli.ts` advisory, unchanged by this PR.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: gateway zombie shutdown that left rh-bot offline 2h13m on 2026-05-31. With this PR the watchdog forces `process.exit(0)` so launchd reaps the PID, and `/healthz` returns 503 during shutdown so the next supervisor cycle does not defer to a draining instance.

Real environment tested: live deployed openclaw gateway on rh-bot.lan, build SHA 23804e610c (incident reproducer). The deployed bundle is the exact code that exhibited the bug; the proof script exercises both the deployed predicate and the patched predicate against real HTTP servers, plus the watchdog state machine.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
scp /tmp/proof-zombie-amittell.mjs alexm@rh-bot.lan:/tmp/proof-zombie-amittell.mjs
ssh alexm@rh-bot.lan 'cd /tmp && node proof-zombie-amittell.mjs'
ssh alexm@rh-bot.lan 'curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:18789/healthz'
```

Evidence after fix:

```
== gateway zombie-shutdown fix proof ==
host: rh-bot.lan
node: v25.8.2
date: 2026-06-01T04:28:32.289Z

[PASS] case1 deployed handler returns 200 mid-shutdown so old preflight defers to zombie :: accepted=true
[PASS] case2 patched predicate (=== 200) rejects 503 as unhealthy :: accepted=false
[PASS] case3 patched predicate accepts a healthy 200 response :: accepted=true
[PASS] case4 watchdog calls exitProcess(0) after timeout :: exited=true; warnMessages=1
[PASS] case4 watchdog warn log names the timeout in ms :: gateway shutdown completed but node process still alive after 50ms; forcing process.exit(0)
[PASS] case5 cancelling the watchdog prevents the forced exit :: exited=false
[PASS] case6a healthz returns 200 ok:true when running :: {"statusCode":200,"body":{"ok":true,"status":"live"}}
[PASS] case6b healthz returns 503 live:false phase:shutting_down when shutdown started :: {"statusCode":503,"body":{"live":false,"phase":"shutting_down"}}

PROOF SUMMARY  passed=8  failed=0
```

Live deployed gateway healthz hit (confirms the case 1 handler shape the proof script reproduces):

```
$ curl -s -w "status=%{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:18789/healthz
status=200
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

Observed result after fix: case 1 confirms the deployed handler shape responsible for the incident. Cases 2 and 3 confirm the patched probe predicate. Cases 4 and 5 confirm the watchdog forces exit on the deployed Node runtime and stops cleanly when cancelled. Cases 6a and 6b confirm the healthz state machine returns the canonical 200 ok body when running and the 503 `{ live: false, phase: "shutting_down" }` body the moment the shutdown flag is set.

What was not tested: a true end-to-end shutdown of a real OpenClaw gateway on rh-bot with the new bundle (requires deployment of this branch); the long-running TCP keepalive vs telegram-fetch handle-leak scenario, which the watchdog handles by design (any stray handle keeps the loop alive past the watchdog timeout, and the watchdog forces exit regardless of the specific handle source).

target.title: feat(dreaming): expose shadow-trial scoring in reports
target.body:
Expose the PR3 shadow-trial scoring signal in the report artifact while keeping the Dreaming path report-only.

This keeps the scoring helper private and only adds report metadata when a caller supplies `candidateScore`:
- base score
- shadow-trial delta
- final review score
- rejected-by-shadow-trial flag
- `scoring action: report-only` beside the existing `promotion action: report-only`

The PR also keeps scored and unscored default report files distinct by including the normalized candidate score in the report hash.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Shadow-trial reports now expose the scoring signal from PR3 as report metadata only, without introducing policy decisions or durable memory writes.

Real environment tested:
Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `feature/dreaming-report-scoring`, using a temporary workspace with a real `MEMORY.md`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
git diff --check
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts docs/concepts/dreaming.md
node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/concepts/dreaming.md
./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts
pnpm check:test-types
node --import tsx - <<'EOF'
import fs from 'node:fs/promises';
import os from 'node:os';
import path from 'node:path';
import { writeDreamingShadowTrialReport } from './extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.ts';

const workspaceDir = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-pr4-shadow-report-'));
const memoryPath = path.join(workspaceDir, 'MEMORY.md');
const before = '# Memory\n\nExisting durable memory.\n';
await fs.writeFile(memoryPath, before, 'utf-8');
const common = {
  candidate: 'The user wants release updates with exact verification and risk notes.',
  trialPrompt: 'Prepare a release readiness note.',
  baselineOutcome: 'Mentions tests passed without the exact command.',
  candidateOutcome: 'Includes exact verification, risk notes, and no private data.',
  verdict: 'helpful',
  reason: 'The candidate improves review quality without changing durable memory.',
  riskFlags: ['no secret exposure'],
  evidenceRefs: ['memory/2026-06-01.md#L28'],
  workspaceDir,
  nowMs: Date.parse('2026-06-01T04:10:00.000Z'),
  timezone: 'Asia/Riyadh',
};
const unscored = await writeDreamingShadowTrialReport(common);
const scored = await writeDreamingShadowTrialReport({ ...common, candidateScore: 0.74 });
const after = await fs.readFile(memoryPath, 'utf-8');
const reportText = await fs.readFile(scored.reportPath, 'utf-8');
console.log(JSON.stringify({
  unscoredReportPath: path.relative(workspaceDir, unscored.reportPath),
  scoredReportPath: path.relative(workspaceDir, scored.reportPath),
  pathsAreDistinct: unscored.reportPath !== scored.reportPath,
  scoreBeforeShadowTrial: scored.scoreBeforeShadowTrial,
  shadowTrialScoreDelta: scored.shadowTrialScoreDelta,
  scoreAfterShadowTrial: scored.scoreAfterShadowTrial,
  scoringAction: scored.scoringAction,
  promotionAction: scored.promotionAction,
  memoryUnchanged: after === before,
  reportContainsFinalReviewScore: reportText.includes('final review score: 0.78'),
  reportContainsNoPromotionMarker: reportText.includes('promotion action: report-only'),
}, null, 2));
EOF
```

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "unscoredReportPath": "memory/dreaming/shadow-trials/2026-06-01/0950174b886a.md",
  "scoredReportPath": "memory/dreaming/shadow-trials/2026-06-01/27901c9c106a.md",
  "pathsAreDistinct": true,
  "scoreBeforeShadowTrial": 0.74,
  "shadowTrialScoreDelta": 0.04,
  "scoreAfterShadowTrial": 0.78,
  "scoringAction": "report-only",
  "promotionAction": "report-only",
  "memoryUnchanged": true,
  "reportContainsFinalReviewScore": true,
  "reportContainsNoPromotionMarker": true
}
```

Observed result after fix:
The scored shadow-trial report includes the final review score and both report-only action markers, while the original `MEMORY.md` contents stay unchanged. Scored and unscored reports are written to separate default report files.

What was not tested:
No live dreaming integration, dry-run promotion policy, audit ledger, config flag, automatic promotion, or durable `MEMORY.md` mutation was tested because those are intentionally outside PR4 scope.

## Tests

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts # 14 passed
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts # 91 passed
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-shadow-trial.test.ts # 62 passed
pnpm check:test-types # passed
```

`pnpm lint --threads=8` still reports two unrelated existing Matrix extension lint findings in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/auto-join.ts` and `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/events.ts`. Direct oxlint on the touched files passed.

If this lands, please preserve Firas Alswihry / @iFiras-Max1 as the contributor attribution.

target.title: feat(docs): add docs_map.md for LLM navigation (fixes #69442)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw has 670+ doc pages across automation/, channels/, cli/, concepts/, gateway/, install/, nodes/, plugins/, providers/, tools/, web/, and more. LLM agents currently have to discover this tree through trial and error. A single `docs_map.md` fixes that by providing a structured outline of every page's headings.

This follows the pattern established by [code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md) ŌĆö a docs map that lets an AI read one file and know exactly what concepts are inside each page.

## Changes

- Add `scripts/generate-docs-map.mjs` ŌĆö walks `docs/`, extracts front-matter title and H2/H3/H4 headings, outputs structured `docs/docs_map.md`
- Add `pnpm docs:map` script to `package.json`
- Generate `docs/docs_map.md` covering 671 pages with 4672 headings

## How to Regenerate

```bash
pnpm docs:map
```

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Adds a docs_map.md navigation file for LLM agents to discover doc pages by heading outline
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node.js, OpenClaw repo at commit a824df2e
- **Steps run after the patch:** Ran `pnpm docs:map` to generate docs/docs_map.md; ran `pnpm build` to verify no build regression
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const c=fs.readFileSync('docs/docs_map.md','utf8'); console.log('pages:', (c.match(/### /g)||[]).length); console.log('headings:', (c.match(/^- /gm)||[]).length); console.log('has_header:', c.startsWith('# Docs Map'));"
pages: 671
headings: 4672
has_header: true
$ grep -n 'generate-docs-map' package.json
1574:    "docs:map": "node scripts/generate-docs-map.mjs",
```
- **Observed result after fix:** docs/docs_map.md is generated with 671 pages and 4672 headings; `pnpm docs:map` script is registered in package.json; `pnpm build` passes
- **What was not tested:** CI workflow integration (auto-regeneration on docs/** push) ŌĆö left as a follow-up since it requires GitHub Actions changes

target.title: fix(agents): project cron tool schemas for providers
target.body:
## Summary
- project runtime tool schemas into provider-facing OpenAPI-friendly shapes by dropping nullable composition wrappers and converting string literal unions to enums
- keep raw TypeBox validation intact so `null` clear sentinels still reach cron runtime normalization
- refresh Codex happy-path prompt snapshots that drifted on current main

Unblocks current CI failures seen on https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/87856 after `d86b6da0120` made cron clear fields nullable in the raw tool schema.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/llm-core/src/validation.test.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts --reporter=dot`: passed, 3 shards / 114 tests
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check`: passed, 7 files current
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 packages/llm-core/src/validation.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/validation.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/codex-dynamic-tools.discord-group.json test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/codex-dynamic-tools.heartbeat-turn.json test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/codex-dynamic-tools.telegram-direct.json test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/discord-group-codex-message-tool.md test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/telegram-direct-codex-message-tool.md test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/telegram-heartbeat-codex-tool.md`: passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs packages/llm-core/src/validation.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/validation.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts`: passed
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`: passed
- AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed`: attempted with `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --label cron-tool-provider-schema-check-changed --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"`, but coordinator returned `active lease limit exceeded: 9/8` before allocation; no lease was created.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: provider-facing tool schemas should avoid OpenAPI 3.0-incompatible nullable/composition shapes, while runtime validation still accepts explicit `null` clear sentinels.

target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2ŌĆō5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #49062

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

- Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post.
- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds.
    - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks.
- Edge cases checked:
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands.
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists.
- What you did not verify:
    - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post
    - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels
    - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for
maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
    - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false
- Files/config to restore:
    - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command."
    - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None.

- Risk:
    - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity.
    - Mitigation:
        - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender.
        - Explicit chat allow is still required.
        - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior.
- Risk:
    - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post.
    - Mitigation:
        - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR.
        - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics.

target.title: fix(update): avoid zsh readonly status in macOS handoffs
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes the macOS update/restart handoff scripts so they no longer assign to a shell variable named `status`.
- `status` is readonly in zsh, so a handoff script interpreted by zsh can stop before restart diagnostics and cleanup finish.
- Keeps the logged wording as `status=...` for operator readability while using `exit_status` internally.

## Linked context
Closes #88809

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: macOS update handoff scripts could fail if interpreted by zsh because `status` is a readonly variable there.
- Real environment tested: local Linux contributor checkout; macOS launchd behavior was verified through generated script assertions and existing launchd/restart helper unit tests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `corepack pnpm test src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- Evidence after fix: the focused test command passed 2 Vitest shards, 34 tests total; new assertions check the generated macOS scripts do not contain `status=` assignments and exit with `$exit_status`.
- Observed result after fix: generated macOS restart handoff templates preserve exit-code handling without assigning to zsh's readonly `status` name.
- What was not tested: a live macOS web UI update/restart flow was not run from this Linux contributor environment.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `zsh` is not installed in this runner, so the direct zsh readonly-variable reproduction from the issue was verified against the generated script source and protected with focused template assertions.

## Tests and validation
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.ts src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `git diff --check`

## Risk checklist
- Did user-visible behavior change? Yes, macOS update/restart handoff scripts avoid a reserved shell variable name.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
- Highest-risk area: preserving restart failure exit-code propagation.
- Mitigation: existing restart-helper and launchd handoff tests still pass, including failure propagation coverage, and new assertions pin the generated macOS script shape.

## Current review state
Ready for maintainer review; no bot or reviewer comments addressed yet.

target.title: feat(agents): auto-trim lean local tools

Summary:
- Adds `agents.*.experimental.localModelLean: "auto"` so lean local tool trimming activates only for models/effective context caps at 64K tokens or smaller.

- Updates config schemas/help, generated baselines, and local-model docs for the new auto mode.

target.title: fix: normalise wiki lint targets
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw wiki lint` can report valid imported wiki links as `broken-wikilink` when they use page titles, slug variants, fragments, or imported source-path suffixes.
- Why it matters: imported evidence should not need provenance-damaging rewrites just to satisfy lint.
- What changed: memory-wiki lint now builds separate case-sensitive path targets and case-insensitive alias targets, including titles, title slugs, source-path suffixes, and fragment/query-stripped targets.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): wiki compile, ingest, page rendering, markdown link extraction, and startup behaviour remain unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #73574
- Related #73722
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: memory-wiki lint compared raw link targets against only exact extensionless relative paths and basenames.
- Missing detection / guardrail: regression coverage did not cover title-style, slug-style, fragment-bearing, source-path suffix, or path-case link variants.
- Contributing context (if known): imported evidence can preserve source-authored link forms that differ from generated wiki filenames.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: existing pages linked by title, slug, fragment-stripped path, and imported source-path suffix do not produce `broken-wikilink` issues.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to lint target indexing and resolution.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw wiki lint` should produce fewer false-positive `broken-wikilink` warnings for imported wiki pages whose links already resolve by title, slug, fragment-stripped path, or imported source-path suffix. Path-style targets remain case-sensitive, so case-mismatched path links still warn.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[[Alpha Database#Evidence]] -> raw target check -> false broken-wikilink

After:
[[Alpha Database#Evidence]] -> alias lookup -> existing page match
[[syntheses/Alpha-DB]] -> path lookup -> broken-wikilink when case differs
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): memory-wiki
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Create a memory-wiki vault containing links such as `[[Alpha Database#Evidence]]`, `[[alpha-database]]`, `[[research notes/Alpha System Overview#Quote]]`, and a case-mismatched path such as `[[syntheses/Alpha-DB]]`.
2. Run memory-wiki lint.
3. Inspect `broken-wikilink` results.

### Expected

- Title, slug, fragment-stripped, and source-path suffix links to existing pages do not warn.
- Case-mismatched path-style links still warn.

### Actual

- Current main only compares raw targets against exact extensionless relative paths and basenames, so valid imported links can be reported as broken.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local command evidence:

- `git diff --check` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts` was attempted locally and with elevated network permissions, but Corepack timed out downloading `pnpm 10.33.0` from the registry.
- `COREPACK_ENABLE_PROJECT_SPEC=0 pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` was attempted with cached `pnpm 10.30.2`, but package fetches from the npm registry timed out after retrying.
- Focused formatter and Vitest proof are deferred to PR CI because local package-manager setup is blocked by registry socket timeouts.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: source-level review confirms lint now separates case-sensitive path matching from case-insensitive alias matching, and indexes titles, title slugs, relative paths, basenames, and imported source-path suffixes.
- Edge cases checked: the new regression keeps case-mismatched path targets warning, addressing the blocker that closed #73722.
- What you did **not** verify: local formatter or Vitest execution, because this machine cannot fetch the required package-manager and dependency tarballs from the npm registry.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: alias matching could suppress warnings for ambiguous non-path titles or slugs.
  - Mitigation: this only suppresses broken-link warnings when a normalised alias matches an existing page key; path-style targets containing `/` remain case-sensitive.

target.title: docs(sms): add Twilio A2P delivery guidance
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a short US A2P / 10DLC delivery section to the SMS channel docs.
- Clarify that OpenClaw SMS setup and US carrier/Twilio A2P approval are separate readiness gates.
- Add troubleshooting guidance for Twilio sends that are accepted by the API but later show `undelivered`, including `30034`, `30909`, and `30923`.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** SMS users can complete OpenClaw webhook/pairing/outbound setup while US carrier delivery still fails because Twilio A2P 10DLC Brand, Campaign, Messaging Service, or sender-number registration is incomplete. This docs change makes that separate delivery gate visible in the SMS channel docs and points users to Twilio's source-of-truth registration/error docs.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `clawsean/sms-a2p-docs`, rebased onto upstream `origin/main` at `7e0d275f7add659d62515c382c112fcf7d84a599`. The edited page is `docs/channels/sms.md`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD -- docs/channels/sms.md
node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/sms.md
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/channels/sms.md
node scripts/docs-link-audit.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the real local checkout after the docs patch:

```text
Docs MDX check passed (1 files, 136ms).
Docs formatting clean (660 files).
checked_internal_links=4544
broken_links=0
```

Also manually reviewed the current Twilio A2P 10DLC overview, registration quickstart, required business/campaign information, and Twilio error pages for `30034`, `30909`, and `30923`. Claude Foreman also reviewed the wording for coverage, flow, and legal-advice risk; verdict was "ready with small edits", and those edits were applied before opening this PR.

**Observed result after fix:** The SMS docs page parses as MDX, passes docs formatting, has no broken internal links, and now contains a concise A2P/10DLC prerequisite section plus a targeted troubleshooting note for Twilio API acceptance followed by carrier delivery failure.

**What was not tested:** I did not send live SMS for this docs-only change. I did not test a Twilio A2P campaign approval flow in the Twilio Console. I did not run full repo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`; this PR only changes one docs page and the targeted docs checks above passed.

## AI-assisted

This PR is AI-assisted. I reviewed the generated wording, checked it against Twilio/OpenClaw docs, and applied a second-pass Claude Foreman review before opening.

target.title: [codex] Read exact X posts via FxTwitter
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a key-free FxTwitter exact-post reader for public X/Twitter status URLs and numeric status IDs.
- Route exact post references through FxTwitter before falling back to xAI-backed broader `x_search`.
- Keep generic X search on the existing xAI auth path and update docs/tests/build guards for the new behavior.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw can read an exact public X/Twitter status URL through `x_search` without requiring an xAI API key for that exact post read.
- **Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw Gateway/Dashboard, built from this patch, restarted under LaunchAgent, listening on `127.0.0.1:18789` with the promoted runtime snapshot.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built OpenClaw, restarted the Gateway, verified `openclaw gateway status --deep`, then invoked the live Gateway HTTP tool endpoint with `curl` against `x_search` using `https://x.com/jack/status/20`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the real Gateway invocation:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "provider": "fxtwitter",
  "mode": "exact_post",
  "statusId": "20",
  "hasExpectedText": true
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The live Gateway returned `provider=fxtwitter`, `mode=exact_post`, `statusId=20`, and confirmed the expected post text was present (`just setting up my twttr`). This proves the exact-post path used FxTwitter and worked on a real running OpenClaw setup.
- **What was not tested:** No known gaps for the exact public post-read path. Broader xAI-backed generic X search remains covered by targeted tests and still requires xAI auth.

## Verification
- `OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm check:changed`
- `PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm test src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts test/scripts/tsdown-build.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/startup-context.test.ts extensions/xai/x-search.test.ts extensions/xai/plugin-registration.contract.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts`

target.title: feat(bedrock): enable native prompt caching for Amazon Nova models (fixes #29707)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Amazon Nova models on Bedrock (nova-pro, nova-lite, nova-micro, nova-premier, nova-2-lite) now support native prompt caching via the Bedrock Converse API. However, OpenClaw hardcodes `cacheRetention: "none"` for all non-Anthropic Bedrock models, which prevents Nova from using caching. This results in ~10x higher API costs for long-running sessions since every turn reprocesses the full system prompt and conversation history.

## Changes Made

- Added `isNovaModel()` detection helper in `register.sync.runtime.ts`
- Modified `baseWrapStreamFn` to skip the no-cache wrapper for Nova models (alongside Claude and app inference profiles)
- Extended `mayNeedCacheInjection` to include Nova models
- Added a Nova-specific cache injection path that injects `cachePoint` blocks directly without requiring AWS API resolution (unlike opaque app inference profiles)
- Updated 4 test assertions that previously expected Nova to get `cacheRetention: "none"`

## Evidence

- **Behavior addressed:** Nova models on Bedrock no longer get forced `cacheRetention: "none"` and instead receive native Converse cache points
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node.js, pnpm build + extension verification suite
- **Steps run after the patch:** Built the project and ran the Amazon Bedrock extension verification suite
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ grep -n "isNovaModel" extensions/amazon-bedrock/register.sync.runtime.ts
93:function isNovaModel(modelId: string): boolean {
94:  return modelId.trim().toLowerCase().startsWith("amazon.nova-");
441:      isNovaModel(modelId)
604:        (isBedrockAppInferenceProfile(modelId) || isNovaModel(modelId)) &&
701:        if (isNovaModel(modelId)) {
```
```
$ node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const c=fs.readFileSync('extensions/amazon-bedrock/register.sync.runtime.ts','utf8'); console.log('has isNovaModel:', c.includes('isNovaModel')); console.log('has cachePoint:', c.includes('cachePoint'));"
has isNovaModel: true
has cachePoint: true
```
```
$ pnpm build
> Build completed successfully
```
- **Observed result after fix:** Nova models pass through without the no-cache wrapper; cache points are injected via `onPayload` with default "short" retention; build completes cleanly
- **What was not tested:** Live Bedrock API calls with Nova models (requires AWS credentials and Nova model access); cache hit/miss behavior on actual API calls

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Nova models on Bedrock no longer get forced `cacheRetention: "none"` and instead receive native Converse cache points
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node.js, pnpm build + extension verification suite
- **Steps run after the patch:** Built the project and ran the Amazon Bedrock extension verification suite
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ grep -n "isNovaModel" extensions/amazon-bedrock/register.sync.runtime.ts
93:function isNovaModel(modelId: string): boolean {
94:  return modelId.trim().toLowerCase().startsWith("amazon.nova-");
441:      isNovaModel(modelId)
604:        (isBedrockAppInferenceProfile(modelId) || isNovaModel(modelId)) &&
701:        if (isNovaModel(modelId)) {
```
```
$ node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const c=fs.readFileSync('extensions/amazon-bedrock/register.sync.runtime.ts','utf8'); console.log('has isNovaModel:', c.includes('isNovaModel')); console.log('has cachePoint:', c.includes('cachePoint'));"
has isNovaModel: true
has cachePoint: true
```
```
$ pnpm build
> Build completed successfully
```
- **Observed result after fix:** Nova models pass through without the no-cache wrapper; cache points are injected via `onPayload` with default "short" retention; build completes cleanly
- **What was not tested:** Live Bedrock API calls with Nova models (requires AWS credentials and Nova model access); cache hit/miss behavior on actual API calls

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

target.title: Make runtime plugin startup stalls name in-flight plugins
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds diagnostic progress snapshots while runtime plugins load.
- Threads runtime plugin progress into embedded-agent and cron execution phase reporting.
- Appends attempted/completed/in-flight plugin ids to cron timeout messages only while the last phase is `runtime_plugins`.

Refs https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/87327 (Ask #1 ŌĆö name the failing plugin)

> Scope note: #87327 lists five asks. This PR implements only **Ask #1** (per-plugin diagnostics naming the in-flight plugin at stall time). Asks #2ŌĆō#5 ŌĆö per-plugin init timeouts, retry-on-phase-stall, a cron failure-rate metric, and confirming the relationship to #86239 ŌĆö are intentionally out of scope and remain open follow-up work, so this uses a non-closing `Refs` reference rather than `Fixes` to avoid auto-closing the broader issue.

## User-facing Bug

When an isolated cron/agent run stalls while loading runtime plugins, the watchdog currently reports only a coarse phase such as `runtime-plugins`. That does not identify which plugin was being resolved, loaded, or registered, so operators cannot tell which plugin to disable, inspect, or report.

## Exact Repro

Source-level reproduction:

1. Arrange runtime plugin loading to report progress for two plugins, with `memory-core` still in `register`.
2. Let the cron pre-execution watchdog time out while the last execution phase is `runtime_plugins`.
3. Before this patch, the timeout text only identified the coarse `runtime-plugins` phase.
4. After this patch, the timeout text includes:

```text
runtime plugins: attempted=[telegram, memory-core]; completed=[telegram]; in-flight=memory-core/register
```

The regression coverage in `src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` exercises this watchdog path.

## Fix Summary

- Added `RuntimePluginLoadProgress` diagnostics types under `src/plugins/runtime/load-diagnostics.ts`.
- Added a loader progress tracker that reports planned plugin ids, completed ids, and the current in-flight plugin phase without letting observer errors affect activation.
- Forwarded the diagnostics through `ensureRuntimePluginsLoaded`, embedded runner phase callbacks, and cron isolated setup.
- Formatted runtime plugin details in cron timeout messages, gated to the `runtime_plugins` phase so stale progress does not pollute later `auth` or `context_engine` timeouts.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: cron/plugin startup stalls now name attempted, completed, and in-flight runtime plugins in timeout diagnostics.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw worktree on macOS, branch `fix/runtime-plugins-stall-diagnostics` at commit `1260a5febd`, rebased onto current `origin/main` (`78c66742ab`); Node `v24.4.1`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
git diff --check
./node_modules/.bin/oxlint <changed files>
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/runtime-plugins.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.runtime-plugins.test.ts src/cron/service/execution-errors.test.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

- `oxlint` passed (exit 0) on all changed files.
- `tsgo` core typecheck (`tsconfig.core.json`) passed (exit 0) after the rebase.
- Focused Vitest passed: cron config `47` tests, agents config `9` tests, plugin loader `153` tests.
- Live formatter output ŌĆö invoked the real production timeout formatters (`src/cron/service/execution-errors.ts`) directly on a stalled `runtime_plugins` execution state (telegram completed, memory-core in-flight at `register`), reproducing the exact operator-facing strings all three cron timeout paths emit:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import { timeoutErrorMessage, setupTimeoutErrorMessage, preExecutionTimeoutErrorMessage } from './src/cron/service/execution-errors.ts'; ..."
timeoutErrorMessage:
  cron: job execution timed out (last phase: runtime-plugins; runtime plugins: attempted=[telegram, memory-core]; completed=[telegram]; in-flight=memory-core/register)
setupTimeoutErrorMessage:
  cron: isolated agent setup timed out before runner start (last phase: runtime-plugins; runtime plugins: attempted=[telegram, memory-core]; completed=[telegram]; in-flight=memory-core/register)
preExecutionTimeoutErrorMessage:
  cron: isolated agent run stalled before execution start (last phase: runtime-plugins; runtime plugins: attempted=[telegram, memory-core]; completed=[telegram]; in-flight=memory-core/register)
contrast (no plugin diagnostics):
  cron: job execution timed out (last phase: runtime-plugins)
```

  The last line is the pre-fix behavior from the linked issue (coarse `runtime-plugins` only); `preExecutionTimeoutErrorMessage` is exactly the issue's reported symptom string (`stalled before execution start`), now naming the in-flight plugin and its phase.
- The cron timeout regression in `src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` drives the watchdog through the `runtime_plugins` phase with two plugins (one still in `register`) and asserts the timeout text includes `attempted`, `completed`, and `in-flight` runtime plugin details. Asserted user-facing output:

```text
Error: isolated agent timed out during runtime-plugins
runtime plugins: attempted=[telegram, memory-core]; completed=[telegram]; in-flight=memory-core/register
```

Observed result after fix: the timeout text names the in-flight plugin and phase instead of only saying `runtime-plugins`.

**Real-loader proof ŌĆö a genuinely slow plugin, observed by the real loader (re-review update)**

Following the [P2] rank-up ask, the in-flight diagnostics are now sourced from the **real `loadOpenClawPlugins` load loop observing a real stalling plugin**, not a hand-built execution state. A throwaway tsx harness wrote two real on-disk plugins and loaded them through the production loader: `progress-a` registers normally; `progress-b`'s `register()` is an intentional busy-wait. The real `runtimePluginLoadProgress` reporter (the exact type the cron/embedded runners wire in) captured the snapshot at the moment the loader was stuck in the slow plugin, and that captured object was fed verbatim into the real `timeoutErrorMessage` / `setupTimeoutErrorMessage` / `preExecutionTimeoutErrorMessage` formatters:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx <harness>
=== loader actually busy-waited in slow plugin register (ms): 3117 ===

REAL loader-emitted in-flight snapshot (watchdog's last-known):
{
  "pluginIds": ["progress-a", "progress-b"],
  "completedPluginIds": ["progress-a"],
  "inFlightPluginId": "progress-b",
  "inFlightPhase": "register"
}

production timeout formatters fed that real snapshot:
timeoutErrorMessage:
  cron: job execution timed out (last phase: runtime-plugins; runtime plugins: attempted=[progress-a, progress-b]; completed=[progress-a]; in-flight=progress-b/register)
setupTimeoutErrorMessage:
  cron: isolated agent setup timed out before runner start (last phase: runtime-plugins; runtime plugins: attempted=[progress-a, progress-b]; completed=[progress-a]; in-flight=progress-b/register)
preExecutionTimeoutErrorMessage:
  cron: isolated agent run stalled before execution start (last phase: runtime-plugins; runtime plugins: attempted=[progress-a, progress-b]; completed=[progress-a]; in-flight=progress-b/register)

pre-fix contrast (coarse phase only, no plugin diagnostics):
  cron: job execution timed out (last phase: runtime-plugins)
```

The loader spent 3,117 ms inside the slow plugin's `register()` ŌĆö i.e. the snapshot was taken while a plugin was genuinely mid-load ŌĆö and every field in the operator-facing message (`attempted` / `completed` / `in-flight=progress-b/register`) originates from the real loader, then the real formatter. The final line is the pre-fix behavior from the linked issue: bare `runtime-plugins` with no way to tell which plugin hung.

This was re-run after rebasing onto current `origin/main` (`78c66742ab`); the single rebase conflict (in `src/agents/runtime-plugins.ts`) was a textual-convergence union ŌĆö main added `forceFullRuntimeForChannelPlugins` and this branch added `runtimePluginLoadProgress` to the same `loadOptions`, both retained. Post-rebase: `oxlint` clean, `tsgo` core typecheck exit 0, and the focused suites pass (`230` tests across runtime-plugins / cron execution-errors / isolated-agent runtime-plugins / loader / timer regression).

What was not tested:

- I did not run this inside a fully deployed gateway/cron daemon with the real watchdog timer firing end-to-end ŌĆö that needs a deployed environment. The hang itself is real (a plugin busy-waiting in `register()` observed by the production loader) and the timeout strings come from the real formatters, but the watchdog's own timer-fire path is covered by the regression test in `src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` rather than a live daemon.
- I did not run the full `pnpm check`; coverage is focused lint, typecheck, and Vitest on the changed surfaces.

## AI-assisted disclosure

This PR was implemented with AI assistance. I reviewed the generated diff, ran the targeted tests above, and addressed the actionable `codex review` finding before preparing this PR.
